perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential I nformation means any data or information that is pr oprietary to or possessed by a P arty and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, ideas, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, speci fications, software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, p resent or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognised as Confide ntial Information by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential I nformation means only trade secrets and financial information directly related to the ILDA-care project that is proprietary to or possessed by a P arty and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, p resent or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: The modification restricts the definition of Confidential Information to only trade secrets and financial information directly related to the ILDA-care project. This contradicts other sections that refer to the broader original definition. For example, Section 3.1 mentions preparations or negotiations, this introduces an in-text contradiction regarding what information is protected under the agreement, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 In the context of discussions, preparations or negotiations, the Disclosing Party may disclose Confidential Information to the Receiving Party. The Receiving Party agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connection with purposes contemplat ed between the Parties in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines Confidential Information broadly. The changed text significantly restricts this definition to only trade secrets and financial information related to the ILDA-care project. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which refers to the disclosure of Confidential Information during 'discussions, preparations or negotiations'. If the definition of Confidential Information is now limited, it's unclear whether information disclosed during preparations or negotiations, but not directly related to trade secrets or ILDA-care project financial info, is protected. This inconsistency creates a clear and direct conflict regarding which information is considered confidential and protected, fitting the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its em ployees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confide ntial quality of the information provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence, the Receiving Party w ill be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non -use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Receiving Party will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees who are directly working on the ILDA-care project and need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Receiving Party will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will not be responsible for the confidentiality if those employees leave their positions.
perturbation.explanation: The modification limits the disclosure of confidential information to employees only directly working on the ILDA-care project and removes the Receiving Party's responsibility for confidentiality if those employees leave their positions. This introduces an in-text contradiction, because clause 3.11 mentions the Receiving Party should give all necessary assistance in connection with any steps which the Disclosing Party may wish to take prevent, stop or obtain compensation, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.11 The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon becoming aware of any breach of confidence by anybody to whom it has disclosed the Confidential Information and give all necessary assistance in connection with any steps which the Disclosing Party may wish to take prevent, stop or obtain compensation for such a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in clause 3.2 limits the Receiving Party's responsibility for confidentiality only to employees 'directly working on the ILDA-care project' and removes responsibility if 'those employees leave their positions.' This directly contradicts clause 3.11, which requires the Receiving Party to provide 'all necessary assistance' to the Disclosing Party in case of any breach of confidence, regardless of whether the employees are still employed. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the Receiving Party is no longer fully responsible for breaches caused by former employees, hindering the 'necessary assistance' as described in 3.11. Therefore, the change introduces inconsistency regarding the Receiving Party's obligations and undermines the enforcement of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 The Parties shall treat all Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 The Parties shall treat all Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the standard of care required for Confidential Information from 'the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information' to 'a reasonable degree of care.' This introduces an in-text contradiction, because Section 3.10 indicates that the Parties agree that the Disclosing Party will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Parties agree that the Disclosing Party will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and tha t the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such a breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the parties treat confidential information with the 'same degree of care' as their own, implying a high and consistent standard. The changed text lowers this standard to a 'reasonable degree of care,' which is subjective and potentially lower than the original requirement. This contradicts section 3.10, which asserts that any breach of confidentiality will cause 'irreparable damage,' implying a higher level of protection than merely 'reasonable' care. The change introduces an inconsistency regarding the expected level of protection for confidential information, leading to a contradiction.
